skill.name: pr
skill.description: Creates specification or implementation pull requests for an SDD workflow using local Git and GitHub CLI commands.
skill.summary: Prepare, review, push, and create pull requests for specification-driven development work.
skill.author: Clionegohan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many backtick, URL, C2, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review shows most are false positives from Markdown examples and Japanese text. The confirmed risk is that the skill guides agents to run git and gh commands that can stage files, commit, push branches, and create pull requests. No evidence found of malware, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden command execution.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Modifying Git and GitHub CLI Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run git add, git commit, git push, and gh pr create. This is legitimate for pull request automation, but it can publish local changes to a remote repository if used without careful review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command list is explicit and semantically tied to creating pull requests. The skill also requires user confirmation, so the behavior is elevated but not malicious.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis labeled many inline code spans and fenced Markdown examples as Ruby shell backtick execution. No executable Ruby code or dynamic shell evaluation is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown syntax or example command blocks, not Ruby code. Review found no script file or runtime backtick execution.

low_findings.title: Example URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Claude Code attribution text and placeholder GitHub pull request examples. No instruction sends secrets, source code, or telemetry to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in templates and sample output. There is no network client code or command that transmits data to an external endpoint.

low_findings.title: C2, Weak Crypto, and Reconnaissance Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The C2 hit maps to acceptance-criteria placeholder text, the weak cryptography hit maps to the Japanese description, and the reconnaissance hit maps to an action-id parameter. No evidence found of command and control, cryptography, or host reconnaissance intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is ordinary documentation content when read in context. There are no cryptographic APIs, C2 endpoints, persistence logic, or system inventory commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Repository Publication Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes git push and gh pr create commands. These commands can publish changes and create GitHub pull requests, so users should inspect diffs and approve the exact PR content first.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly listed in the skill instructions. The risk depends on operator approval and repository permissions rather than hidden malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Specification-driven work often needs consistent pull request titles, summaries, and review checklists. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through Git checks, PR body creation, user confirmation, and GitHub PR creation.
actual_capabilities: Creates PR titles for specification work using the spec prefix.
Creates PR titles for implementation work using the impl prefix.
Builds PR summaries from changed files and action identifiers.
Defines review checklists for specifications, acceptance criteria, and test plans.
Guides use of git status, git diff, git commit, git push, and gh pr create.
Handles common cases such as no changes, existing PRs, and missing GitHub CLI authentication.
limitations: Requires Git and GitHub CLI to be installed and authenticated.
Can publish repository changes when the user approves push and PR commands.
Does not verify test results unless the agent or user runs the tests.
Designed for repositories that follow the referenced SDD workflow.

use_cases.title: Specification Review PR
use_cases.description: Create a structured pull request after finishing phase, task, and action specification files.

use_cases.title: Implementation Review PR
use_cases.description: Prepare an implementation pull request with a summary, changed files, test plan, and acceptance criteria checklist.

use_cases.title: Workflow Handoff
use_cases.description: Standardize PR creation when work is handed from a spec workflow to a review workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic PR
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for the current branch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pr skill to inspect the current branch and draft a pull request. Ask before pushing or creating the PR.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Specification PR
prompt_templates.scenario: Specification files are ready for review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pr skill with type spec for action {action-id}. Summarize the spec files and include review points for user stories, acceptance criteria, dependencies, and scope.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Implementation PR
prompt_templates.scenario: Implementation work is ready for review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pr skill with type impl for action {action-id}. Include changed files, completed acceptance criteria, and a test plan before asking for approval.

prompt_templates.title: Recover from Existing PR
prompt_templates.scenario: A branch may already have a pull request.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pr skill and check whether this branch already has a PR. If one exists, present update and replacement options before taking any remote action.

output_examples.input: Create an implementation PR for the current branch.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports the current branch, proposes an impl title, summarizes changed files, lists a test plan, and asks for confirmation before creating the PR.

output_examples.input: Create a specification PR for action 001-01-01.
output_examples.output: The assistant drafts a spec title, lists generated specification files, adds review questions, and waits for user approval.

output_examples.input: There are no changes on this branch.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that there is nothing to commit and offers to continue work or create a PR from existing commits.

best_practices: Review git diff before allowing commits, pushes, or PR creation.
Run the relevant tests before creating implementation pull requests.
Confirm the base branch, PR title, and body before any remote action.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to push directly to the base branch.
Do not create implementation PRs when acceptance criteria or tests are incomplete.
Do not approve PR creation without reading the generated summary and test plan.

faq.question: Does this skill create pull requests automatically?
faq.answer: It guides PR creation and includes a user confirmation step before creating the PR.

faq.question: What tools does it require?
faq.answer: It expects Git and GitHub CLI to be available in the repository environment.

faq.question: Can it create both spec and implementation PRs?
faq.answer: Yes. It defines separate title patterns and PR bodies for spec and impl work.

faq.question: Does it run tests?
faq.answer: It includes test plan guidance, but tests must be run by the user or agent environment.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses legitimate Git and GitHub commands that can publish local changes to a remote repository.

faq.question: Is there evidence of malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No evidence found of malware, credential theft, prompt injection, or hidden exfiltration.
